questions.stem: In a food chain, which group of organisms makes its own food and forms the base of the chain?
questions.explanation: Producers, mainly green plants and algae, make their own food by photosynthesis and form the base of a food chain. Herbivores and carnivores are consumers that eat other organisms, and decomposers break down dead material.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory ecology text

questions.stem: What is the original source of almost all energy that enters most ecosystems?
questions.explanation: Sunlight is the original energy source for most ecosystems. Producers capture light energy by photosynthesis and convert it into chemical energy in food, which then passes to consumers. Soil, wind, and rock minerals supply materials but not this primary energy input.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory ecology text

questions.stem: Organisms that break down dead plants and animals and return nutrients to the environment are called what?
questions.explanation: Decomposers, such as many bacteria and fungi, break down dead organisms and waste, releasing nutrients back into the soil and recycling them. Producers make food, primary consumers eat producers, and apex predators sit at the top of food chains.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory ecology text

questions.stem: In the food chain grass to grasshopper to frog to snake, what is the grasshopper?
questions.explanation: The grasshopper eats grass, a producer, so it is a primary consumer (the first consumer level). The frog that eats the grasshopper is a secondary consumer, the grass is the producer, and decomposers act on dead matter rather than appearing in this chain.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory ecology text

questions.stem: Roughly what percentage of energy is typically passed from one trophic level to the next in a food chain?
questions.explanation: As a common rule of thumb, only about 10 percent of the energy at one trophic level is passed on to the next. The rest is lost mainly as heat from respiration and in undigested material, which is why food chains usually have only a few levels.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory ecology text

questions.stem: Why do food chains usually have only four or five trophic levels?
options: Because energy is lost at each level, leaving little for higher levels
Because predators cannot find prey above that level
Because sunlight cannot reach higher levels
questions.explanation: Energy decreases at each step of a food chain because much of it is lost as heat and in waste. After several levels there is too little energy left to support another level, which limits the length of most food chains.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory ecology text

questions.stem: Which term describes all the populations of different species living and interacting in the same area?
questions.explanation: A community is made up of all the interacting populations of different species in an area. A population is one species in an area, a species is a group of similar interbreeding organisms, and an individual is a single organism.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory ecology text

questions.stem: Which term refers to a group of organisms of the same species living in a particular area at the same time?
questions.explanation: A population is a group of individuals of one species living in the same area at the same time. A community includes many species, an ecosystem adds the non-living environment, and the biosphere is all life on Earth.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory ecology text

questions.stem: Which of the following best defines an ecosystem?
options: A community of organisms together with their non-living environment
A single population of one species
Only the plants in an area
questions.explanation: An ecosystem is a community of living organisms interacting with each other and with the non-living (abiotic) parts of their environment, such as air, water, and soil. It is more than just the plants or a single population.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory ecology text

questions.stem: In an ecosystem, which of the following is an abiotic (non-living) factor?
questions.explanation: Temperature is an abiotic, or non-living, factor of the environment. Bacteria, grass, and insects are living organisms and so are biotic factors. Abiotic factors also include light, water, and soil.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory ecology text

questions.stem: What does a food web show that a single food chain does not?
options: The exact mass of each organism
Only one predator and one prey
Many interconnected feeding relationships in a community
questions.explanation: A food web shows how many food chains in a community link together, giving a fuller picture of the feeding relationships among species. A single food chain shows only one pathway of energy, while a web shows the many interconnected pathways.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory ecology text

questions.stem: During photosynthesis, green plants take in carbon dioxide and release which gas?
questions.explanation: In photosynthesis, plants use carbon dioxide and water with light energy to make glucose, releasing oxygen as a by-product. This released oxygen is essential for the respiration of most living things. Nitrogen, methane, and hydrogen are not the gas released here.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory ecology text

questions.stem: In the carbon cycle, which process returns carbon dioxide to the atmosphere from living organisms?
questions.explanation: Respiration by plants, animals, and other organisms breaks down food and releases carbon dioxide back into the atmosphere. Photosynthesis does the reverse by removing carbon dioxide, while condensation and sedimentation are physical processes not part of this carbon return.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory ecology text

questions.stem: Which process in the water cycle changes liquid water into water vapor that rises into the atmosphere?
questions.explanation: Evaporation changes liquid water into water vapor that rises into the air, driven mainly by heat from the Sun. Condensation forms clouds when vapor cools, precipitation is rain or snow falling, and infiltration is water soaking into the ground.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory ecology text

questions.stem: Nitrogen-fixing bacteria are important in the nitrogen cycle because they do what?
options: Convert nitrogen gas from the air into compounds plants can use
Break down water into hydrogen and oxygen
Turn nitrogen compounds back into nitrogen gas only
questions.explanation: Nitrogen-fixing bacteria convert nitrogen gas from the atmosphere into nitrogen compounds such as ammonia, which plants can absorb and use to build proteins. This step is essential because most organisms cannot use nitrogen gas directly.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory ecology text

questions.stem: A close, long-term relationship in which both species benefit is best described as which type of interaction?
questions.explanation: Mutualism is a relationship in which both species benefit, such as bees gaining nectar while pollinating flowers. In predation one organism eats another, in competition both are harmed as they vie for resources, and in parasitism one benefits while the host is harmed.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory ecology text

questions.stem: A relationship in which one organism lives on or in a host and harms it while benefiting itself is called what?
questions.explanation: In parasitism, the parasite benefits while the host is harmed, as with a tapeworm living in an animal's gut. Mutualism benefits both, commensalism benefits one without affecting the other, and producer is not a type of interaction between two organisms.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory ecology text

questions.stem: Which of the following is a density-dependent factor that can limit the growth of a population?
options: Competition for food among members of the population
questions.explanation: Competition for food becomes more intense as a population grows denser, so its effect depends on population density, making it a density-dependent factor. A volcanic eruption, flood, or cold snap affects a population regardless of its density, so these are density-independent factors.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory ecology text

questions.stem: The largest population size that an environment can support over the long term, given its resources, is called what?
questions.explanation: Carrying capacity is the maximum population size that an environment can sustain over time, set by limits such as food, water, and space. Biomass is the total mass of living matter, birth rate is how fast new individuals are added, and biodiversity is the variety of life.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory ecology text

questions.stem: An organism that eats only plants is best described as what?
questions.explanation: A herbivore feeds only on plants or other producers. A carnivore eats other animals, an omnivore eats both plants and animals, and a decomposer breaks down dead organic matter, so none of those fits an organism that eats only plants.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory ecology text

questions.stem: A pyramid of energy in an ecosystem usually has which shape, and why?
options: Upright, because available energy decreases at higher trophic levels
Inverted, because energy increases at higher levels
Circular, because energy is fully recycled
Perfectly rectangular, because energy stays constant
questions.explanation: A pyramid of energy is upright, wide at the producer base and narrowing upward, because usable energy is lost at each trophic level and less remains for the levels above. Energy does not increase upward, stay constant, or get fully recycled within the chain.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory ecology text

questions.stem: The particular role or way of life of a species in its ecosystem, including how it gets food and interacts with others, is called its what?
questions.explanation: A niche is the role a species plays in its ecosystem, including what it eats, where it lives, and how it interacts with other organisms. A habitat is the physical place where it lives, a population is a group of one species, and a biome is a large climatic region.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory ecology text

questions.stem: Which statement about energy flow and nutrient cycling in an ecosystem is correct?
options: Both energy and nutrients are lost and never reused
Both energy and nutrients are completely recycled
Nutrients flow one way while energy is recycled
Energy flows through and is lost, while nutrients are recycled
questions.explanation: Energy flows through an ecosystem in one direction and is gradually lost as heat, so it must be constantly resupplied by the Sun. Nutrients such as carbon and nitrogen, by contrast, are cycled and reused through biogeochemical cycles.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory ecology text

questions.stem: The physical place where an organism naturally lives is best described as its what?
questions.explanation: A habitat is the natural physical environment where an organism lives, such as a pond or a forest floor. A niche is its functional role, a community is all the interacting species, and a trophic level is its feeding position in a food chain.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory ecology text
